comment.body:
For anyone who is new, the limitations of Substack leads to a lot of confusion. My 2 cents on how to approach using Alma's Substack is below.... pro tip is to BOOKMARK the links in your browser:

** Review each Weekly Post every Monday (https://stochvoltrader.substack.com/s/market-analysis?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)
** Review each Daily Post at beginning of the day (same link as above)

** Have two windows open for Chats everyday:
---- New Daily Chat set up each day 
---- Trading Signals Chat (Alma provides trading signals occasionally; set up on February 3 soon after Alma opened up the Substack; link is https://substack.com/chat/2679129/post/5ec8d5aa-9fac-4e6d-aef1-3f6a28632ae4)

** Educate yourselves with these chats and posts by reading through them, maybe even more than once:
---- Ongoing Q&A Chat (set up on February 26; https://substack.com/chat/2679129/post/398075ab-2aac-41d1-bf37-8fda43ccf9aa) 
---- Options Depth Heat Map Overview (set up on February 5; https://substack.com/chat/2679129/post/2aab643b-d095-4c00-8b42-4f35379fb002)
---- Educational Posts (https://stochvoltrader.substack.com/s/educational-posts) 

Every so often I will copy and re-paste this post into chats when new folks jump in. Hope this helps!!!

comment.body:
your logic is good, but not entirely correct.
1) yes, the purpose of that call is to create a synthetic exposure against the new collar, so there is no extra synthetic short exposure against their long equity portfolio exposures (in their brochoure they put it down clear, what % they hedge: no more and no less, never)
2) the new collar is set at 2PM... you have 2 hours until market close. If you buy OTM call, your delta decays fast = no delta hedge against the collar.
if you buy ATM, same problem bcs you are exposed to the price swings.
If you buy ITM, you have positive charm, your deltas will approach 1, at expo.

So the answer is a deep ITM 0DTE call

comment.body:
@Trader McGraw , with all due respect, what I’m trying to explain to people is that the Greeks are overemphasized. 
Many people like to talk about them because they get to use fancy-sounding words, and it makes them seem really smart while they desperately try to explain everything/every market moves with the greeks.
Indeed, dealer flow is a very significant factor in the market, especially in the microstructure. But it’s not the only influencing factor, and it’s not always important.

Yesterday’s rally was not some "gamma/charm rally", that’s simply nonsense. 
I wrote in the weekly post, without mentioning any greeks, that funds are strategically scalping the long side and would be buying on Monday. And that’s exactly what happened. Also showed and explained how market is underpricing the real risk bcs it doesn't see whats going on (Do you even read my posts btw?)
Dealer flow is reflexive, and volatility plays a very significant role, which people can only understand by monitoring different tenors. Many analysts lack this aspect.

comment.body:
@Trader McGraw , with all due respect, what I’m trying to explain to people is that the Greeks are overemphasized. 
Many people like to talk about them because they get to use fancy-sounding words, and it makes them seem really smart while they desperately try to explain everything/every market moves with the greeks.
Indeed, dealer flow is a very significant factor in the market, especially in the microstructure. But it’s not the only influencing factor, and it’s not always important.

Yesterday’s rally was not some "gamma/charm rally", that’s simply nonsense. 
I wrote in the weekly post, without mentioning any greeks, that funds are strategically scalping the long side and would be buying on Monday. And that’s exactly what happened. Also showed and explained how market is underpricing the real risk bcs it doesn't see whats going on (Do you even read my posts btw?)
Dealer flow is reflexive, and volatility plays a very significant role, which people can only understand by monitoring different tenors. Many analysts lack this aspect.

comment.body:
My objective of being here is to learn how to read MM and options flow data to better improve my intraday trading success and, potentially, improve swing trading success up to 3 months out (if applicable).

@Terrence Chan made this post yesterday about your service and I agree that this encapsulates my experience so far. Here it is:

“My 2 cents. The consistent thing that you can be sure is actionable that you will get from this service are the intraday levels and the dealer flow analysis.

Anything beyond a day but probably a good read on the week are her weekly pivots and then goes to longer positioning like JPM collar or iron condor man. 

And then outside of this - what you’re getting is interpreting of the fed actions, the geo politician actions, and possibly quarter end options expiry/vixperation analysis. 

It’s like the layers to the onion. There are some core things that are very much actionable. But the speculative outer layers you have to understand are stabs at predictions the future that Alma is making her best interpretation of 
for our benefit.”

I agree that your analysis seems to be most actionable on an intraday basis. Yet you are saying that the macro understanding of the larger story is the most important. My issue here is, like Terrence alluded to, that the “outer layers” of the onion are the most “speculative” and “subjective”. It’s the least quantifiable analysis that can be applied accurately from a timing and price perspective. And therefore is the least actionable.

So are we “muddying up the waters” by talking about this when your analysis is most applicable and actionable on a shorter time frame?

I thought you are here to teach us to fish… yet there are times where you want to be treated as an analyst service where your skills cannot be replicated or taught directly. Just seems like sometimes we’re are getting lost in what the purpose of this Substack is in the first place.

I can appreciate your geopolitical analysis and your commentary on what funds are doing, but unless you continue to provide that for as long as I am trading, it really doesn’t help me outside of the next year or so you choose to have this Substack. I guess you are showing to think critically or to try to piece the puzzle together, but that is so broad and subjective that somebody could get caught in their own feedback loop. You just said you have acquaintances in the industry to bounce your ideas off of to see if you are on the right track. But, as I mentioned, many of your subscribers don’t. 

Does this make sense?

comment.body:
My objective of being here is to learn how to read MM and options flow data to better improve my intraday trading success and, potentially, improve swing trading success up to 3 months out (if applicable).

@Terrence Chan made this post yesterday about your service and I agree that this encapsulates my experience so far. Here it is:

“My 2 cents. The consistent thing that you can be sure is actionable that you will get from this service are the intraday levels and the dealer flow analysis.

Anything beyond a day but probably a good read on the week are her weekly pivots and then goes to longer positioning like JPM collar or iron condor man. 

And then outside of this - what you’re getting is interpreting of the fed actions, the geo politician actions, and possibly quarter end options expiry/vixperation analysis. 

It’s like the layers to the onion. There are some core things that are very much actionable. But the speculative outer layers you have to understand are stabs at predictions the future that Alma is making her best interpretation of 
for our benefit.”

I agree that your analysis seems to be most actionable on an intraday basis. Yet you are saying that the macro understanding of the larger story is the most important. My issue here is, like Terrence alluded to, that the “outer layers” of the onion are the most “speculative” and “subjective”. It’s the least quantifiable analysis that can be applied accurately from a timing and price perspective. And therefore is the least actionable.

So are we “muddying up the waters” by talking about this when your analysis is most applicable and actionable on a shorter time frame?

I thought you are here to teach us to fish… yet there are times where you want to be treated as an analyst service where your skills cannot be replicated or taught directly. Just seems like sometimes we’re are getting lost in what the purpose of this Substack is in the first place.

I can appreciate your geopolitical analysis and your commentary on what funds are doing, but unless you continue to provide that for as long as I am trading, it really doesn’t help me outside of the next year or so you choose to have this Substack. I guess you are showing to think critically or to try to piece the puzzle together, but that is so broad and subjective that somebody could get caught in their own feedback loop. You just said you have acquaintances in the industry to bounce your ideas off of to see if you are on the right track. But, as I mentioned, many of your subscribers don’t. 

Does this make sense?
